quote: The real voyage of discovery consists not in seeking new landscapes, but in having new eyes.
through_line: The week ends where it started: with the question of what we're navigating toward. Destination marketing, after all its reinvention, still depends on someone wanting to go somewhere they haven't been. And what drives a four-year-old to keep exploring after a wrong answer — what researchers call learning progress — turns out to be the same thing that drives a serious practitioner deeper. Not certainty. The pull of what isn't yet understood.

articles.title: What is destination marketing in 2025 and beyond? DMO meaning and evolution for the future
articles.pitch: As AI reshapes every layer of the travel discovery process, this piece asks a foundational question: what does destination marketing actually mean now? Madden Media traces how the DMO role has evolved — from print guides to digital content to something more resembling place stewardship in an era of algorithmic travel planning.

articles.title: Exploration in 4-year-old children is guided by learning progress and novelty
articles.pitch: A 2025 study found that young children don't just chase novelty — they actively select activities that offer the most learning progress, and their interest in growing correlates with stronger actual outcomes. It quietly reframes curiosity as something more than exploration: a drive toward the edge of understanding, where the most real learning lives.

articles.title: Biology, Buddhism, and AI: Care as the driver of intelligence
articles.pitch: An unusual convergence paper: the author argues that what makes minds intelligent — in organisms, in practice, and potentially in AI — is not processing power but care. Drawing on Buddhist philosophy and enactivist biology, it frames intelligence as inherently relational and oriented toward the alleviation of suffering. A piece that sits differently after a week of reading.

articles.title: The brain's hidden drain
articles.pitch: The brain's glymphatic system — its waste-clearance infrastructure — is most active during rest, flushing the metabolic byproducts of a busy mind. New research suggests what's called the resting brain is doing something far more than cleanup: it's processing in ways the working brain simply cannot. A quiet piece that changes the way you read a pause.
